explicit_text: I was born in the 27th year of the Meiji period, which was 1894. My father often told us about his experiences during those times, especially how Christianity was not freely accepted then. The Meiji period was a time of great change in Japan, and it shaped much of my family's life and faith.
implicit_text: I was born in the 27th year of the era marked by Japan's rapid modernization, which was 1894. explicit_text: My father was born in Tokyo when it was still called Yedo, and later I came to Tokyo myself for schooling and nursing training. Tokyo was lively even back then, and I remember attending a hospital there where I became a midwife and nurse. It was also in Tokyo that my father had many clients and where our family was connected to the Christian community.

explicit_text: My father was one of the first graduates of what is now Chiba Medical University, back when it was just founded. He chose medicine over the military, despite initially trying to pass the military academy exam. His medical career started in Kemigawa and later Kisarazu, where he worked as a prison and courthouse doctor.

explicit_text: Yamaoka Tesshu was my father's teacher at his private school. My father tried to pass the military academy exam while studying under him but failed the physical due to pleurisy. Yamaoka Tesshu advised my father to keep his faith private at that time, reflecting the challenges Christians faced then.

explicit_text: Our family was affiliated with the Nihon Kirisuto Kyokai, which is a Presbyterian church. We attended services there and had missionaries visit from time to time. This church was a central part of our Christian life in Japan.

explicit_text: My father found 'Pilgrim's Progress' at a bookstore in Nihonbashi and read it before the Bible. It was written in classic Japanese and introduced him to Christian ideas during a time when faith was not freely accepted. This book was important in his spiritual journey.

explicit_text: After some years practicing medicine, my father moved to Kisarazu where he worked as a prison doctor and courthouse doctor. Kisarazu was a town that later became a city, and my father was well known there as a medical practitioner. It was part of his career progression after facing difficulties as a Christian doctor.

explicit_text: When I was about ten or eleven years old, the Russo-Japanese War broke out. Russia was Greek Catholic, and because of that, Christians like us were persecuted in Japan during that time. I remember the hostility we faced, such as stones being thrown at me on the way to school and damage to our home during festivals.

explicit_text: The World's Sunday School Convention was held in Poke around the time I came to America. Mr. Igarashi, who later became my husband, attended as a delegate. This event helped bring us together and was part of the reason I eventually moved to America.
